source.title: Pharmacotherapies for sleep disturbances in dementia

confidence_rationale: Cochrane systematic review, well-conducted. Key finding: orexin antagonists (suvorexant/lemborexant) probably improve total sleep time (MD +28.2 min, moderate certainty) and reduce WASO in mild-moderate AD; trazodone 50mg improves total sleep time and efficiency in moderate-severe AD (low certainty, small trial n=30). Melatonin shows little or no effect. This mixed pattern is partially consistent with MECH-177: orexin antagonist and trazodone are exactly the two architecture-favorable agents predicted to have DPM potential, while melatonin shows weaker effects. However, the outcomes measured are sleep improvement, not cognitive progression -- MECH-177 predicts disease modification, which this Cochrane review does not test directly.
failure_signatures: Melatonin (up to 10mg) shows little or no effect on any major sleep outcome in AD -- this partially challenges the ramelteon/melatonin-ER disease-modifying candidate prediction in MECH-177, though melatonin-ER formulation and ramelteon (MT1/MT2 agonist vs. non-selective melatonin) are different from standard melatonin.

No RCT evidence for benzodiazepine or Z-drug use in dementia sleep -- the Cochrane review explicitly notes this gap, meaning MECH-174's predictions about harm from GABAergic agents are not directly contradict by RCT evidence (no data, not negative data).
Trial durations (2-4 weeks for most) are too short to test MECH-177's disease-modification prediction -- sleep improvement in 4 weeks does not establish prevention of pipeline collapse over years.
The ramelteon study (n=74) shows no effect on sleep outcomes -- though the review notes the limited evidence from an unpublished sponsor summary.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Orexin antagonists (suvorexant/lemborexant) probably increase total nocturnal sleep time by 28 min and decrease WASO by 16 min in mild-to-moderate AD (moderate certainty). Trazodone 50mg for 2 weeks improves total sleep time by 42 min and sleep efficiency by 8.5% in moderate-severe AD (low certainty). Melatonin up to 10mg: little or no effect. Ramelteon 8mg: no important effect on sleep. No RCTs of benzodiazepines or Z-drugs in dementia.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-177 predicts architecture-preserving agents (orexin antagonists, trazodone) should have disease-modifying potential via pipeline protection in MCI/early AD. The Cochrane finding that orexin antagonists improve sleep in mild-moderate AD and trazodone improves sleep in moderate-severe AD is consistent with the predicted efficacy pattern. The lack of benzodiazepine/Z-drug RCTs in dementia is consistent with MECH-174 predictions (clinicians may avoid them for the same reasons MECH-174 predicts), but is not evidence of harm.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The Cochrane outcomes (total sleep time, sleep efficiency, WASO) are sleep quality metrics, not dementia progression metrics. MECH-177's disease-modification claim requires showing slowed cognitive decline, which requires much longer trials with cognitive endpoints. The current evidence supports efficacy for sleep improvement, not DPM. The melatonin null finding is a partial challenge to the circadian-restoration DPM candidate, though melatonin-ER and ramelteon have different pharmacodynamics from standard melatonin.
mapping.source_context: Cochrane systematic review; 9 RCTs, n=649 total; participants had dementia (mostly AD) with identified sleep disturbance; primary outcomes via actigraphy or PSG; median trial duration 2-4 weeks.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Cochrane). Mapping fidelity limited because sleep improvement outcomes don't test disease-modification prediction of MECH-177. Evidence is indirect for DPM.
